attributes.stem:
A 29-year-old man is being treated as an inpatient due to bloody diarrhoea. He presented with a seven day history of acute onset diarrhoea accompanied with cramping abdominal pain, fatigue, and a fever of 37.8C. He has had six loose bowel actions per day with frank blood. Initial investigations show a CRP of 135, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 42, with mild leukocytosis. A stool sample is negative for clostridioides difficile toxins. An abdominal X-ray excludes toxic megacolon.

A sigmoidoscopy reveals markedly erythematous and inflamed colonic mucosa with decreased typical superficial vascular markings. There are superficial ulcers with streaks of blood along the mucosal lining of the descending and sigmoid colon.

He was commenced on intravenous hydrocortisone however his symptoms failed to improve after 72 hours.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Continue IV steroids for a further 72 hours
attributes.explanation:
This is a case of acute severe ulcerative colitis. Truelove and Witt's criteria is the common tools that is used to characterise the severity of ulcerative colitis. According to Truelove and Witt's criteria, patients presenting with >6 episodes of bloody stool in a day accompanied with either a fever of 37.8C, tachycardia, ESR >30mmHg, or Hb <10.5gm/dL is characterised as having severe ulcerative colitis. Whilst most cases of acute severe colitis will respond to medical intervention, there is still a significant colectomy rate and it is sensible to get a surgical opinion early in the management.

First line management of severe ulcerative colitis is intravenous corticosteroid. Most patients with acute severe ulcerative colitis (ASUC) achieve remission with corticosteroid alone, however 25% of patients may need additional medical therapy. It is also important to give adequate fluid replacement, monitor patient's electrolytes and nutrition, and rule out infectious causes as part of the management plan.

If symptoms fail to improve after three days of steroid therapy, infliximab is the recommended next line treatment. Infliximab is a monoclonal antibody to TNF-a which plays an important role in the inflammation process of ulcerative colitis. It also increases the likelihood of corticosteroid-free remission.

Upadacitinib is a JAK inhibitor medication that is associated with potential benefit for patients with moderate ulcerative colitis, however it is not the mainstay therapy for treatment of steroid refractory acute severe ulcerative colitis.

Adding IV antibiotics is not advisable. Ulcerative colitis is an autoimmune disease, therefore without any signs of co-infection, antibiotics play a minimal role in the management of ulcerative colitis.

Continue IV steroids for a further 72 hours is not recommended in this case. It is advised to start infliximab if patient does not improve within three days. Research has also shown that continuing corticosteroid therapy alone carry minimal benefit after 7-10 days of therapy. Corticosteroid is also associated with potential serious systemic side effects.

Vedolizumab is not recommended in the treatment of acute severe ulcerative colitis. Vedolizumab is a treatment option for patients with active inflammatory bowel disease who have failed conventional medical therapy.
learningPoints: Infliximab is the recommended next line treatment for patients with steroid refractory acute severe ulcerative colitis.
Acute severe ulcerative colitis is characterised by frequent bloody stools, fever, and elevated inflammatory markers.
Early surgical consultation is advisable in the management of acute severe ulcerative colitis due to the risk of colectomy.
references: Danese S, Vermiere S, Zhou W, et al. Upadacitinib as induction and maintenance therapy for moderately to severely active ulcerative colitis: results from three phase 3, multicentre, double-blind, randomised trials. Lancet 2022; 399:2113-28. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35644166/

Lamb C. A., Kennedy N. A., Raine T., et al. 3029. British Society of Gastroenterology consensus guidelines on the management of inflammatory bowel disease in adults, Gut, 68(Suppl 3), pp. s1-s106. Available from: https://gut.bmj.com/content/68/Suppl_3/s1

Gajendran M, Loganathan P, Jimenez G, Catinella AP, Ng N, Umapathy C, Ziade N, Hashash JG. A comprehensive review and update on ulcerative colitis. Dis Mon. 2019 Dec;65(12):100851. doi: 10.1016/j.disamonth.2019.02.004. Epub 2019 Mar 2. PMID: 30837080. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30837080/

Kedia S, Ahuja V, Tandon R. Management of acute severe ulcerative colitis. World J Gastrointest Pathophysiol. 2014 Nov 15;5(4):579-88. doi: 10.4291/wjgp.v5.i4.579. PMID: 25401001; PMCID: PMC4231522. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25401001/

Järnerot G, Hertervig E, Friis-Liby I, Blomquist L, Karlén P, Grännö C, Vilien M, Ström M, Danielsson A, Verbaan H, Hellström PM, Magnuson A, Curman B. PMID: 15940615. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15940615/
